Healthy.description: The strawberry plant (garden strawberry) is in excellent condition. Leaves are bright green, without spots, plaque, or deformations. The bush is vigorous, actively growing, flowering, and fruiting. Berries are large, evenly colored, without rot or mechanical damage.
symptoms: Rich green color of leaf blades
No spots, plaque, curling, or necrosis
Large, healthy berries with good taste
Healthy.treatment: Continue standard care: regular root watering, timely removal of runners (if not needed for propagation), mulching beds, applying balanced fertilizers according to the growth stage.
Healthy.prevention: Observe crop rotation (return strawberries to the same place no earlier than after 3-4 years). Use healthy seedlings. Maintain optimal soil moisture (without stagnant water). Carry out preventive treatments with biological products in spring and autumn (Fitosporin, Trichodermin). Remove weeds and plant debris. Fertilize with potassium and phosphorus in time to boost immunity.

Bacterial Burn.description: Bacterial disease. Yellow or brown irregular spots appear on leaves, growing quickly, as if after contact with fire.
Bacterial Burn.treatment: Remove affected leaves. Spray with copper-containing preparations. If severely affected, destroy the plant.
Bacterial Burn.prevention: Avoid injuring leaves, treat cuts with garden pitch, prevent waterlogging.

Bacterial Root Cancer.description: Bacterial disease. Ugly growths appear on the plant's roots, disrupting nutrition.
Bacterial Root Cancer.treatment: Dig up and burn diseased plants. Treat the soil with copper sulfate solution or Fitolavin.
Bacterial Root Cancer.prevention: Buy healthy seedlings, disinfect roots before planting, observe crop rotation.

Bacteriosis.description: Bacterial disease caused by Corynebacterium fascians. Affects weakened plants. Bushes stop developing, leaves and stems become deformed, flowers grow on thick deformed pedicels.
symptoms: Deformation of leaf blades and stems
Bacteriosis.treatment: Remove and burn diseased plants. Treat remaining bushes with bactericides. Improve soil health.
Bacteriosis.prevention: Maintain plant immunity, avoid overcrowding, apply phosphorus-potassium fertilizers.

Rot.description: Fungal disease caused by Sclerotinia libertiana. Berries rot and become covered with fluffy white mold.
Rot.treatment: Destroy affected berries and leaves. Spray plants with fungicides. Normalize humidity.
Rot.prevention: Avoid excessive watering, ventilate plantings, remove plant debris.

White Spot (Ramulariasis).description: Fungal disease caused by Mycosphaerella fragariae and Ramularia tulasnei. Brown spots appear on leaves, then light spots with a dark border.
symptoms: Drying and loss of spot center
White Spot (Ramulariasis).treatment: Remove diseased leaves. Spray with systemic fungicides. Repeat after 10-14 days if necessary.
White Spot (Ramulariasis).prevention: Use healthy seedlings, thin out plantings, avoid waterlogging.

Brown Spot.description: Fungal disease caused by Marssonina petontillae. Leaves become covered with brown spots with a light center. Appears during fruit set and ripening.
Brown Spot.treatment: Remove affected leaves. Treat bushes with fungicides. If severely infected, destroy plants.
Brown Spot.prevention: Observe crop rotation, avoid overcrowding, remove weeds, mulch soil.

Verticillium Wilt.description: Fungal disease caused by Verticillium dahliae. Starts from the roots, then spreads to leaves, runners, and fruits. Develops especially quickly on sandy soils.
Verticillium Wilt.treatment: Water the soil and plants with fungicides. Remove severely affected bushes with a clump of soil.
Verticillium Wilt.prevention: Use healthy seedlings, observe crop rotation, add organic matter to improve soil.

Witches' Broom.description: Viral disease, also known as 'witches' broom'. Main signs: small flower stalks, lack of berries, and abundant shoot overgrowth.
Witches' Broom.treatment: No cure. Immediately remove and burn all affected bushes along with roots.
Witches' Broom.prevention: Purchase certified seedlings, disinfect soil, remove carriers (leafhoppers, aphids).

Root Rot (Armillariosis).description: Fungal disease caused by Armillaria mellea (honey fungus). The fungus settles on roots, preventing nutrition and growth. Lower leaves die off, harvest is poor.
Root Rot (Armillariosis).treatment: Dig up and burn diseased plants. Treat soil with liming and fungicides.
Root Rot (Armillariosis).prevention: Do not plant strawberries after trees or in old areas with honey fungus, use improved soil.

Leaf Edge Yellowing.description: Viral disease. Central leaves turn yellow at the edges, bushes become dwarfed and pressed to the ground.
Leaf Edge Yellowing.treatment: No cure. Remove diseased specimens. Treat soil against nematodes and viruses.
Leaf Edge Yellowing.prevention: Use healthy planting material, control nematodes (potential carriers).

Mottle.description: Viral disease. The presence of the virus is indicated by light green mottles (small spots) on leaves. The plant weakens, does not form runners or ovaries.
Mottle.treatment: No cure. Destroy diseased bushes. Improve plantation health.
Mottle.prevention: Buy verified seedlings, observe crop rotation, control aphids and leafhoppers.

Mosaic (Xanthosis).description: Viral disease. Yellow spots resembling a mosaic appear on leaf blades. Bush growth stops, runners and flower stalks dry up, leaves become deformed.
symptoms: Drying of runners and flower stalks
Mosaic (Xanthosis).treatment: No cure. Remove and burn affected plants. Disinfect the soil.
Mosaic (Xanthosis).prevention: Use resistant varieties, control sucking pests, do not take runners from diseased bushes.

Leaf Curl.description: Viral disease. Leaf blades curl, shrink, and roll towards the edges. Edges turn yellow and dry. Flowering usually does not occur.
Leaf Curl.treatment: No cure. Diseased plants must be immediately removed and burned.
Leaf Curl.prevention: Buy healthy seedlings, control carriers (aphids, mites), disinfect tools.

Powdery Mildew.description: Fungal disease caused by Uncinula necator. The entire bush becomes covered with white coating, leaves dry out, plant loses frost resistance.
symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves, stems, and berries
Powdery Mildew.treatment: Spray with sulfur-containing fungicides. Remove severely affected leaves.
Powdery Mildew.prevention: Do not overfeed with nitrogen, ensure air circulation, carry out preventive treatments.

Non-infectious Damage.description: Damage caused by adverse environmental conditions, not pathogens. Includes freezing, hail damage, deficiency or excess of nutrients, water balance disturbance.
symptoms: Chlorosis, edge burn from element deficiency
Non-infectious Damage.treatment: Restore agricultural practices: adjust watering, fertilize, cover for winter, protect from hail with netting. Remove severely damaged parts.
Non-infectious Damage.prevention: Proper care, frost protection, balanced fertilizing, selection of zoned varieties.

Leaf Scorch.description: Fungal disease caused by Diplocarpon earlianum. Brown spots enlarge, leaves become 'scorched'.
symptoms: Complete browning and drying of leaves
Leaf Scorch.treatment: Remove diseased leaves. Treat with copper-containing preparations. Thin out plantings.
Leaf Scorch.prevention: Avoid waterlogging, fertilize with potassium and phosphorus, carry out preventive spraying in spring.

Greening of Petals.description: Viral disease (phytoplasmosis). Leaves become small, yellowish-green with mosaic coloring. Flower petals shrink, acquire light green color, sepals overgrow. Berries do not set.
Greening of Petals.treatment: No cure. Remove all affected plants. Control carriers (leafhoppers).
Greening of Petals.prevention: Use healthy seedlings, destroy weeds, spray with insecticides against leafhoppers.

Drying (Ramulariosis).description: Fungal disease caused by Ramularia grevilleana. Leaves become covered with rusty spots, dry out, plant languishes.
Drying (Ramulariosis).treatment: Remove diseased leaves. Spray with fungicides. In advanced cases, uproot plants.
Drying (Ramulariosis).prevention: Maintain low humidity, ventilate plantings, apply phosphorus-potassium fertilizers.

Rhizoctonia Rot.description: Fungal disease caused by Rhizoctonia solani. Affects roots, causing bushes to not receive water and nutrition.
Rhizoctonia Rot.treatment: Water soil with fungicides. Transplant healthy bushes to a new area. Destroy diseased ones.
Rhizoctonia Rot.prevention: Disinfect soil before planting, do not add fresh manure, observe crop rotation.

Gray Mold.description: Fungal disease caused by Botrytis cinerea. Berries develop patches with grayish fluffy mold coating, fruits rot. Develops with excessive moisture.
Gray Mold.treatment: Remove all rotten berries. Treat bushes with fungicides. Reduce watering. Mulch beds.
Gray Mold.prevention: Avoid overcrowding, remove weeds, harvest berries on time, avoid water getting on fruits.

Angular Leaf Spot.description: Bacterial disease. Begins with watery damage on the underside of the leaf, then dark green angular spots form, exuding bacteria. Lesions merge into reddish spots with a chlorotic halo.
symptoms: Watery dots on underside of leaf
Angular Leaf Spot.treatment: Remove diseased leaves. Treat with bactericides. Destroy heavily infected bushes.
Angular Leaf Spot.prevention: Use healthy runners, do not overhead water, carry out preventive spraying.

Fusarium Wilt.description: Fungal disease where the pathogen enters through roots, causing marginal leaf necrosis. Leaf blades change color at edges, then dry out. Develops in overcrowded plantings with high acidity.
symptoms: Marginal leaf necrosis (yellowing and drying of edges)
Fusarium Wilt.treatment: Water soil with fungicide solution. Remove severely affected bushes. Deacidify soil.
Fusarium Wilt.prevention: Avoid overcrowding, control soil acidity (pH 5.5-6.5), use healthy planting material.

Black Rot.description: Fungal disease caused by fungi of the genus Mycosphaerella. Fruits become covered with black moldy coating and rot. Berries with mechanical damage are more often affected.
Black Rot.treatment: Collect and destroy diseased berries. Treat bushes with contact fungicides. Avoid injuring fruits.
Black Rot.prevention: Harvest carefully, avoid damage, maintain cleanliness in beds.

Black Spot (Anthracnose).description: Fungal disease caused by Colletotrichum acutatum. Manifests as dark specks on leaves, stems, and fruits. When roots are affected, bushes die.
symptoms: Dark depressed spots on petioles, runners, and berries
Black Spot (Anthracnose).treatment: Remove and burn severely affected plants. Treat remaining bushes with copper-containing fungicides.
Black Spot (Anthracnose).prevention: Buy certified seedlings, disinfect soil, observe crop rotation.
